LABEL: ER Send - Airway Emergency
BODY: Based on your symptoms, you need immediate emergency evaluation. Please go to the nearest emergency room or call 911. Key concerns: {{red_flag_labels}}. Do not eat or drink until evaluated.

LABEL: Urgent Evaluation Needed
BODY: Your symptoms suggest you need prompt medical evaluation. Concerns identified: {{red_flag_labels}}. Please visit urgent care or your doctor within 24 hours. {{immediate_actions}}

LABEL: High Centor - Strep Likely
BODY: Your Modified Centor Score is {{centor_score}}/5, suggesting a higher likelihood of strep throat. We recommend a rapid strep test. If positive, antibiotics will be prescribed. In the meantime: rest, stay hydrated, and use OTC pain relief as needed.

LABEL: Moderate Centor - Testing Recommended
BODY: Your Modified Centor Score is {{centor_score}}/5. A rapid strep test is recommended to determine if antibiotics are needed. Continue supportive care: warm fluids, throat lozenges, and OTC pain relief.

LABEL: Persistent Symptoms
BODY: Your sore throat has lasted {{duration_days}} days, which is longer than typical viral pharyngitis. We recommend: mono test (EBV), CBC with differential, and possible ENT referral if symptoms continue.

LABEL: Recurrent Strep
BODY: You report recurrent strep throat episodes. We recommend: throat culture (not just rapid test), ENT referral to discuss tonsillectomy criteria, and review of close contacts for carrier state.

LABEL: Low Centor - Viral Likely
BODY: Your Modified Centor Score is {{centor_score}}/5, suggesting a viral cause is most likely. Antibiotics are not recommended. Supportive care: rest, warm fluids, honey for cough, OTC pain relief (acetaminophen or ibuprofen), and throat lozenges. Return if symptoms worsen or persist beyond 10 days.

LABEL: Default Sore Throat
BODY: Based on your evaluation, we recommend routine follow-up for your sore throat. Continue supportive care and return if symptoms worsen.

LABEL: ER Send - Ear Emergency
BODY: Based on your symptoms, you need immediate emergency evaluation. Please go to the nearest emergency room. Key concerns: {{red_flag_labels}}. {{immediate_actions}}

LABEL: Urgent Ear Evaluation
BODY: Your ear symptoms require prompt medical evaluation. Concerns: {{red_flag_labels}}. Please visit urgent care or your doctor within 24 hours. {{immediate_actions}}

LABEL: Otitis Externa (Swimmer's Ear)
BODY: Your symptoms are consistent with otitis externa (outer ear infection). Please see your doctor for ear drops (typically antibiotic/steroid combination). In the meantime: keep your ear dry, avoid inserting anything into the ear canal, and use OTC pain relief as needed.

LABEL: Acute Otitis Media
BODY: Your symptoms suggest a possible middle ear infection (acute otitis media). Please see your doctor for evaluation. They may prescribe antibiotics depending on severity. Use OTC pain relief (acetaminophen or ibuprofen) for comfort. Apply a warm compress to the affected ear.

LABEL: TMJ-Related Ear Pain
BODY: Your ear pain appears related to your jaw joint (TMJ). Self-care recommendations: soft diet for 1-2 weeks, avoid wide jaw opening and gum chewing, apply warm compresses to the jaw, OTC anti-inflammatory medication (ibuprofen). If not improving in 2 weeks, see your dentist or doctor.

LABEL: Eustachian Tube Dysfunction
BODY: Your symptoms suggest eustachian tube dysfunction (blocked feeling without infection). Try: Valsalva maneuver (gently blow with nose pinched), chewing gum, yawning, OTC decongestant (pseudoephedrine) for a few days. If symptoms persist beyond 2 weeks, see your doctor.

LABEL: Salivary Gland Infection
BODY: Your symptoms suggest a possible salivary gland infection. Please visit urgent care for evaluation. Stay well hydrated, apply warm compresses to the swollen area, and massage the gland gently. Antibiotics may be needed.

LABEL: Ear Trauma Evaluation
BODY: Your ear injury with drainage needs evaluation to check for eardrum damage. Please visit urgent care within 24 hours. Keep the ear dry, do not insert anything into the ear canal, and do not use ear drops until evaluated.

LABEL: Default Earache
BODY: Based on your evaluation, we recommend follow-up with your doctor for your ear symptoms. Use OTC pain relief as needed and return if symptoms worsen.

LABEL: ER Send - Cough Emergency
BODY: Based on your symptoms, you need immediate emergency evaluation. Please go to the nearest emergency room. Key concerns: {{red_flag_labels}}. {{immediate_actions}}

LABEL: Urgent Cough Evaluation
BODY: Your cough symptoms require prompt medical evaluation. Concerns: {{red_flag_labels}}. Please visit urgent care or your doctor within 24 hours. {{immediate_actions}}

LABEL: Hemoptysis Emergency
BODY: Coughing up blood requires immediate emergency evaluation. Please go to the nearest ER for CT chest and labs. Do not delay.

LABEL: Asthma Exacerbation
BODY: Your persistent cough with wheezing and known asthma suggests an asthma exacerbation. Please see your doctor for: PFTs or peak flow, inhaler technique review, possible steroid burst, and updated asthma action plan.

LABEL: COPD Evaluation
BODY: Your persistent cough with known COPD history needs evaluation. Please see your doctor for: spirometry, chest X-ray, sputum culture if productive, and medication review.

LABEL: Possible Respiratory Infection
BODY: Your cough lasting {{cough_duration}} weeks with fever suggests a possible respiratory infection. Please see your doctor for: chest X-ray, CBC, and possible antibiotics depending on findings.

LABEL: Chronic Cough Workup
BODY: Your cough has lasted {{cough_duration}} weeks. Recommended workup: chest X-ray, PFTs, consider CT chest, evaluate for GERD/post-nasal drip/ACE inhibitor use, and possible pulmonology referral.

LABEL: Post-Nasal Drip Cough
BODY: Your cough appears related to post-nasal drip (upper airway cough syndrome). Self-care recommendations: nasal saline rinses 2-3 times daily, OTC antihistamine (cetirizine or loratadine), nasal steroid spray (fluticasone), stay hydrated, and elevate your head while sleeping. If not improving in 2-3 weeks, see your doctor for further evaluation.

LABEL: GERD-Related Cough
BODY: Your cough appears related to acid reflux (GERD). Self-care recommendations: avoid eating 3 hours before bedtime, elevate the head of your bed 6-8 inches, avoid trigger foods (spicy, acidic, fatty), OTC antacid (omeprazole or famotidine) for 2-4 weeks. If not improving, see your doctor for further evaluation and possible referral to GI.

LABEL: Default Persistent Cough
BODY: Based on your evaluation, we recommend follow-up with your doctor for your persistent cough. Monitor for worsening symptoms and return if you develop shortness of breath, chest pain, or cough up blood.

LABEL: ER Send - Chest Pain Emergency
BODY: Based on your symptoms, you need immediate emergency evaluation. Please call 911 or go to the nearest emergency room immediately. Key concerns: {{red_flag_labels}}. {{immediate_actions}} Do not drive yourself.

LABEL: Urgent Chest Pain Evaluation
BODY: Your chest pain symptoms require prompt medical evaluation. Concerns: {{red_flag_labels}}. Please visit urgent care or your doctor within 24 hours. {{immediate_actions}}

LABEL: Possible Pericarditis
BODY: Your chest pain pattern (worse lying flat, improved leaning forward, recent viral illness) suggests possible pericarditis. Please see your doctor urgently for: ECG, echocardiogram, inflammatory markers (CRP/ESR), and troponin.

LABEL: Chest Pain with Respiratory Infection
BODY: Your chest pain with fever and cough suggests a possible respiratory infection or pneumonia. Please visit urgent care for: chest X-ray, CBC, and evaluation for antibiotics.

LABEL: GERD-Related Chest Pain
BODY: Your chest pain appears related to acid reflux (GERD). Recommendations: avoid eating 3 hours before bedtime, elevate head of bed, avoid trigger foods, OTC antacid trial (omeprazole or famotidine) for 2-4 weeks. See your doctor if not improving.

LABEL: Anxiety-Related Chest Pain
BODY: Your chest pain pattern is consistent with stress/anxiety-related chest discomfort. Recommendations: stress reduction techniques, breathing exercises, regular physical activity. See your doctor for further evaluation and to discuss treatment options.

LABEL: Musculoskeletal Chest Pain
BODY: Your chest pain appears musculoskeletal in origin (reproducible with palpation, no cardiac warning signs). Self-care: OTC anti-inflammatory (ibuprofen or naproxen), ice/heat application, avoid aggravating movements. See your doctor if pain persists beyond 1-2 weeks.

LABEL: Default Chest Pain
BODY: Based on your evaluation, we recommend follow-up with your doctor for your chest pain. Return immediately if you develop shortness of breath, sweating, radiation to arm/jaw, or fainting.

LABEL: ER Send - Dizziness Emergency
BODY: Based on your symptoms, you need immediate emergency evaluation. Please call 911 or go to the nearest emergency room. Key concerns: {{red_flag_labels}}. {{immediate_actions}}

LABEL: Urgent Dizziness Evaluation
BODY: Your dizziness symptoms require prompt medical evaluation. Concerns: {{red_flag_labels}}. Please visit urgent care or your doctor within 24 hours. {{immediate_actions}}

LABEL: Possible Hypoglycemia
BODY: Your dizziness with diabetes history and sweating suggests possible hypoglycemia. Check your blood sugar immediately. If low (below 70 mg/dL), consume 15g fast-acting carbohydrates. See your doctor urgently to adjust your diabetes medications.

LABEL: Orthostatic Dizziness
BODY: Your dizziness worsens on standing and you may be dehydrated. Recommendations: increase fluid intake, rise slowly from sitting/lying, avoid prolonged standing, and see your doctor for orthostatic vital signs and medication review.

LABEL: Medication-Related Dizziness
BODY: Your dizziness may be related to a recently started medication. Do not stop any medication without consulting your doctor. Please see your doctor to review your medications and consider alternatives.

LABEL: Vestibular Neuritis
BODY: Your dizziness pattern (constant spinning, recent viral illness) suggests vestibular neuritis. See your doctor for: vestibular exam, possible MRI to rule out stroke, and treatment with vestibular suppressants and physical therapy (vestibular rehabilitation).

LABEL: Benign Positional Vertigo (BPPV)
BODY: Your dizziness pattern (brief spinning with position changes) is consistent with BPPV. Self-care: Epley maneuver (your doctor can demonstrate), avoid rapid head movements, sleep with head slightly elevated. Most cases resolve within weeks. See your doctor if symptoms persist.

LABEL: Default Dizziness
BODY: Based on your evaluation, we recommend follow-up with your doctor for your dizziness. Return immediately if you develop weakness on one side, trouble speaking, severe headache, or fainting.

LABEL: ER Send - Abdominal Emergency
BODY: Based on your symptoms, you need immediate emergency evaluation. Please go to the nearest emergency room. Key concerns: {{red_flag_labels}}. {{immediate_actions}} Do not eat or drink until evaluated.

LABEL: Urgent Abdominal Pain Evaluation
BODY: Your abdominal symptoms require prompt medical evaluation. Concerns: {{red_flag_labels}}. Please visit urgent care or your doctor within 24 hours. {{immediate_actions}}

LABEL: Possible Diverticulitis
BODY: Your left lower abdominal pain with fever suggests possible diverticulitis. Please visit urgent care for: CT abdomen, CBC, and evaluation for antibiotics. Stay on a clear liquid diet until evaluated.

LABEL: Possible Kidney Stone
BODY: Your flank-to-groin pain pattern suggests a possible kidney stone. Please visit urgent care for: CT abdomen without contrast, urinalysis, and pain management. Stay well hydrated. Strain your urine to catch any stones.

LABEL: Gastroenteritis
BODY: Your symptoms are consistent with gastroenteritis (stomach bug). Self-care: clear liquids for 24 hours then advance diet slowly, OTC anti-diarrheal (loperamide) if needed, avoid dairy and fatty foods. Return if symptoms worsen, you develop fever, or cannot keep fluids down for 24 hours.

LABEL: Default Abdominal Pain
BODY: Based on your evaluation, we recommend follow-up with your doctor for your abdominal pain. Return immediately if pain worsens, you develop fever, vomiting blood, or bloody/black stools.

LABEL: ER Send - Urinary Emergency
BODY: Based on your symptoms, you need immediate emergency evaluation. Please go to the nearest emergency room. Key concerns: {{red_flag_labels}}. {{immediate_actions}}

LABEL: Urgent Urinary Evaluation
BODY: Your urinary symptoms require prompt medical evaluation. Concerns: {{red_flag_labels}}. Please visit urgent care or your doctor within 24 hours. {{immediate_actions}}

LABEL: Possible Pyelonephritis
BODY: Your flank pain with fever suggests a possible kidney infection (pyelonephritis). Please visit urgent care for: urinalysis, urine culture, CBC, and evaluation for antibiotics. Stay well hydrated.

LABEL: UTI in Pregnancy
BODY: Urinary symptoms during pregnancy require prompt evaluation and treatment. Please see your OB provider or visit urgent care for: urinalysis, urine culture, and safe antibiotic treatment.

LABEL: Male UTI Evaluation
BODY: UTI symptoms in males require medical evaluation to rule out prostatitis or other causes. Please see your doctor for: urinalysis, urine culture, and possible prostate evaluation.

LABEL: Possible Kidney Stone
BODY: Your colicky flank-to-groin pain pattern suggests a possible kidney stone. Please visit urgent care for: CT abdomen, urinalysis, and pain management. Stay well hydrated and strain your urine.

LABEL: Possible STI Evaluation
BODY: Your symptoms suggest a possible sexually transmitted infection rather than a UTI. Please see your doctor for: STI testing (GC/CT/RPR), urinalysis, and appropriate treatment. Partner notification may be needed.

LABEL: Simple Cystitis
BODY: Your symptoms are consistent with a simple bladder infection (cystitis). Self-care: increase fluid intake, OTC urinary analgesic (phenazopyridine) for comfort. See your doctor or use a telehealth visit for antibiotics if symptoms persist beyond 2 days.

LABEL: Default UTI Evaluation
BODY: Based on your evaluation, we recommend follow-up with your doctor for your urinary symptoms. Return if you develop fever, flank pain, or worsening symptoms.

LABEL: ER Send - Testicular Emergency
BODY: Based on your symptoms, you need immediate emergency evaluation. Please go to the nearest emergency room. Key concerns: {{red_flag_labels}}. {{immediate_actions}} Do not delay.

LABEL: Urgent Testicular/Prostate Evaluation
BODY: Your symptoms require prompt medical evaluation. Concerns: {{red_flag_labels}}. Please visit urgent care or your doctor within 24 hours. {{immediate_actions}}

LABEL: Acute Prostatitis
BODY: Your symptoms suggest acute prostatitis. Please visit urgent care for: urinalysis, urine culture, and antibiotics. Avoid prostatic massage. Stay hydrated and use NSAIDs for pain.

LABEL: Epididymitis - STI Related
BODY: Your symptoms suggest epididymitis possibly related to an STI. Please visit urgent care for: STI testing, urinalysis, and antibiotic treatment. Partner notification and treatment may be needed.

LABEL: Epididymitis - Enteric
BODY: Your symptoms suggest epididymitis. Please see your doctor for: urinalysis, urine culture, and antibiotic treatment. Scrotal support and NSAIDs may help with comfort.

LABEL: Referred Pain - Kidney Stone
BODY: Your flank-to-groin pain with blood in urine suggests a possible kidney stone with referred pain. Please visit urgent care for: CT abdomen, urinalysis, and pain management.

LABEL: Testicular Trauma Evaluation
BODY: Your testicular injury with swelling needs evaluation. Please visit urgent care for: scrotal ultrasound and urology follow-up. Apply ice, use scrotal support, and take OTC pain relief.

LABEL: Possible Varicocele/Hydrocele
BODY: Your chronic mild discomfort worsened by standing is consistent with a varicocele or hydrocele. See your doctor for: scrotal ultrasound and urology referral if bothersome. Scrotal support may help.

LABEL: Default Testicular Pain
BODY: Based on your evaluation, we recommend follow-up with your doctor for your symptoms. Return immediately if you develop sudden severe pain, fever, or scrotal skin changes.

LABEL: ER Send - Pelvic Emergency
BODY: Based on your symptoms, you need immediate emergency evaluation. Please go to the nearest emergency room or call 911. Key concerns: {{red_flag_labels}}. {{immediate_actions}}

LABEL: Urgent Pelvic Pain Evaluation
BODY: Your pelvic symptoms require prompt medical evaluation. Concerns: {{red_flag_labels}}. Please visit urgent care or your doctor within 24 hours. {{immediate_actions}}

LABEL: Possible PID
BODY: Your symptoms suggest pelvic inflammatory disease. Please visit urgent care for: pelvic exam, STI testing, and antibiotics. Partner treatment is important to prevent reinfection.

LABEL: Possible Ruptured Ovarian Cyst
BODY: Your symptoms are consistent with a ruptured ovarian cyst. Please visit urgent care for: pelvic ultrasound and evaluation. Most resolve on their own, but monitoring is important.

LABEL: UTI with Pelvic Pain
BODY: Your urinary symptoms suggest a possible UTI contributing to your pelvic pain. See your doctor for: urinalysis, urine culture, and appropriate treatment.

LABEL: Possible Fibroids
BODY: Your heavy bleeding with pelvic pressure suggests possible uterine fibroids. See your doctor for: pelvic ultrasound and GYN referral. Iron supplementation may be helpful if you have heavy bleeding.

LABEL: Possible Endometriosis
BODY: Your cyclical pelvic pain pattern suggests possible endometriosis. See your doctor for: GYN referral, discussion of hormonal management, and pain management options.

LABEL: Benign Pelvic Cramps
BODY: Your mild pelvic discomfort without danger signs appears benign. Self-care: OTC pain relief (ibuprofen or naproxen), heat application, rest. See your doctor if symptoms worsen or persist.

LABEL: Default Pelvic Pain
BODY: Based on your evaluation, we recommend follow-up with your doctor for your pelvic pain. Return immediately if you develop severe pain, fever, heavy bleeding, or dizziness.

LABEL: ER Send - Headache Emergency
BODY: Based on your symptoms, you need immediate emergency evaluation. Please call 911 or go to the nearest emergency room. Key concerns: {{red_flag_labels}}. {{immediate_actions}}

LABEL: Urgent Headache Evaluation
BODY: Your headache symptoms require prompt medical evaluation. Concerns: {{red_flag_labels}}. Please visit urgent care or your doctor within 24 hours. {{immediate_actions}}

LABEL: Possible Giant Cell Arteritis
BODY: Your age, new headache pattern, and associated symptoms raise concern for giant cell arteritis. Please see your doctor urgently for: ESR and CRP blood tests, possible temporal artery biopsy, and consideration of steroid therapy.

LABEL: Carbon Monoxide Concern
BODY: Multiple household members with similar headache and nausea symptoms raises concern for carbon monoxide exposure. Please leave your home immediately and go to the nearest ER. Call 911 or the fire department.

LABEL: Migraine Management
BODY: Your headache pattern (unilateral, throbbing, with light sensitivity) is consistent with migraine. See your doctor for: migraine-specific treatment, prevention strategies if frequent, and to rule out secondary causes.

LABEL: Cluster Headache
BODY: Your headache pattern (orbital pain with tearing and restlessness) is consistent with cluster headache. See your doctor for: oxygen therapy prescription, triptans for acute attacks, and preventive treatment options.

LABEL: Tension Headache
BODY: Your headache pattern is consistent with tension-type headache. Self-care: OTC pain relief (acetaminophen or ibuprofen), stress management, adequate sleep, and regular meals. See your doctor if headaches become frequent or severe.

LABEL: Default Headache
BODY: Based on your evaluation, we recommend follow-up with your doctor for your headache. Return immediately if you develop the worst headache of your life, fever with neck stiffness, weakness, or vision changes.

LABEL: Sinus Emergency
BODY: Your sinus symptoms require emergency evaluation. Signs suggest a possible serious complication. Go to the emergency room immediately. Do not delay.

LABEL: Bacterial Sinusitis
BODY: Your symptoms suggest bacterial sinusitis. You should be seen at urgent care for evaluation and possible antibiotic treatment. Bring a list of your current medications and allergies.

LABEL: Sinusitis Follow-Up
BODY: Your sinus symptoms have been persisting. We recommend scheduling a visit with your primary care provider for further evaluation and management.

LABEL: Viral Sinusitis Self-Care
BODY: Your symptoms are consistent with viral sinusitis. Self-care measures: saline nasal rinses, steam inhalation, adequate hydration, OTC decongestants and pain relievers. See a doctor if symptoms worsen or last beyond 10 days.
